statement: Sunny and Moonbeam are at the two ends of a straight 1-mile walking trail. They begin walking toward each other along the trail at the same time. Sunny walks at 4 miles per hour, and Moonbeam walks at 3 miles per hour. They meet at point A and continue their respective walks. At point B, Sunny turns around and starts walking in the same direction as Moonbeam. They both reach the end of the trail at the same time. Compute the number of miles in AB.
solution: The locations of points A and B can be independently computed as follows. Sunny and Moonbeam first meet after they have collectively walked 1 mile, and this occurs after 1 = 1 hours. Hence point A is 4+3 7 4 · 1 = 4 miles from Sunny’s starting point. 7 7 4 mph 3 mph Sunny Moonbeam 4 A 3 7 7 The second meeting occurs after Moonbeam has walked 1 mile, the length of the entire trail. Because Sunny walks at 4 the rate of Moonbeam, it follows that Sunny walked a total of 4/3 miles. Thus B is 2/3 miles from Sunny’s starting point. 4 mph 3 mph Sunny Moonbeam 2 B Therefore the number of miles in AB is |2/3 − 4/7| = 2/21.
summary: Tests computing meeting points of two walkers using distance-rate-time relationships.

statement: A square is circumscribed about the graph of |x|+|y| = 20 and is inscribed in the graph of |x|+|y| = 25. Compute the area of this square. √ √
solution: The graph G of |x| + |y| = 20 is a square with side length 20 2. The graph G of |x| + |y| = 25 is a 1 √ 2 square with side length 25 2. Let the inscribed square S in the problem statement have side length s. Corresponding sides of G and G are parallel to each other. Hence the angles between a side of G and the 1 2 1 two sides of S touching it are congruent to the angles between a side of S and the two sides of G touching it. It follows that the similarity ratio between G and S equals that between S and G . This implies 1 2 √ 25 2 s = √ , s 20 2 so the desired area is √ √ s2 = 20 2 · 25 2 = 1000. Alternate Solution: As before, let the inscribed square S in the problem statement have side length s. Impose a coordinate system where one of the vertices of S is (a, b), with a + b = 25 and a, b > 0. Then the other three vertices are (−b, a), (−a, −b), and (b, −a). The line passing through (a, b) and (−b, a) must pass through (0, 20), so it follows that b − 20 a − 20 = =⇒ a2 + b2 = 20(a + b) = 500. a − 0 −b − 0 The side length of the square is given by » » √ s = (a + b)2 + (b − a)2 = 2(a2 + b2) = 2 · 500, so s2 = 1000. Note that solving for (a, b) from a + b = 25 and a2 + b2 = 500 yields the solutions {a, b} = ¶ √ © 5 (5 ± 15) . √ √
summary: Tests recognizing |x|+|y|=c as a square and using similarity or coordinates to find an inscribed square's area.

statement: Suppose that △ABC has tan A + tan B = 2025 and cot A + cot B = 1976. Given that tan C = q r, where q is a rational number and r is a squarefree integer, compute r.
solution: Notice that 1 1 tan B + tan A 2025 1976 = + = = , tan A tan B tan A tan B tan A tan B which implies tan A tan B = 2025 . Because tan C = tan(180◦ − (A + B)) = − tan(A + B), it follows that tan A + tan B 2025 2025 tan C = − = − = · 1976. 1 − tan A tan B 1 − 2025/1976 49 √ √ √ Thus tan C = 45 · 1976 = 90 494. Because 494 = 2 · 13 · 19 is squarefree, it follows that r = 494. 7 7 Alternate Solution: Rearranging the fractions 2025 tan A + tan B = = cot B + cot A = 1976, tan A tan B tan A tan B it follows that tan A tan B = 2025 . For any triangle ABC, recall the identity tan A + tan B + tan C = tan A tan B tan C. √ √ √ This leads to 2025 + tan C = 2025 tan C, thus tan C = 2025·1976 and tan C = 45 1976 = 90 494. Again, 1976 49 7 7 r = 494.

statement: Consider the cubic polynomials p(x) = x3 + 7x2 + 14x + 8 and q(x) = ax3 + bx2 + cx + d. Suppose that the graphs of y = p(x) and y = q(x) are symmetric across the point (4, 5). Compute the unique real solution to the equation q(x) + 130 = 0.
solution: Instead of solving q(x) = −130, reflect the line y = −130 across the point (4, 5) to get the line y = 140. First consider the equation p(x) = 140, which is equivalent to x3 + 7x2 + 14x − 132 = 0, and note that p(3) = 140, which implies that x = 3 is a solution. This is the only real solution because x3 + 7x2 + 14x − 132 = (x − 3)(x2 + 10x + 44), and for all real x, x2 + 10x + 44 = (x + 5)2 + 19 > 0. Finally, reflecting the point (3, 140) in (4, 5) yields (5, −130), so x = 5 is the desired solution. Alternate Solution: Note that p(x) = x3 + 7x2 + 14x + 8 = (x + 1)(x2 + 6x + 8) = (x + 1)(x + 2)(x + 4). Because the graphs of y = p(x) and y = q(x) are symmetric about the point (4, 5), it follows that p(4 − x) + (cid:0) (cid:1) (cid:0) (cid:1) q(4 + x) = 10; that is, (4, 5) is the midpoint of the segment with endpoints 4 − x, p(4 − x) and 4 + x, q(4 + x) . Therefore q(x + 4) = 10 − p(4 − x) = 10 − (5 − x)(6 − x)(8 − x) = (x − 5)(x − 6)(x − 8) + 10, so q(x) = (x − 9)(x − 10)(x − 12) + 10. Thus (x − 9)(x − 10)(x − 12) + 10 = −130, which implies (x − 9)(x − 10)(x − 12) = −140 = (−4)(−5)(−7), hence x − 9 = −4, so x = 5.
summary: Tests point symmetry of cubic graphs and polynomial factorization to find a unique real root.

statement: The sport of llama slabber requires four players from a team for each game. For any two games that a team plays in a season, they must use exactly two of the same players in both games, and no two players can play together in more than two games. Let G be the maximum number of games that a team can play in a season, and let P be the minimum number of players that a team needs in order to play G games in a season. Compute the ordered pair (G, P ). ←→
solution: Denote the players by capital letters A, B, and so on. Call the players in the first game of the season A, B, C, and D. Each subsequent game after the first must use a distinct subset of those four players. Hence there are a maximum of (cid:0)4(cid:1) = 6 additional games, thus G ≤ 1 + 6 = 7. To give an example that uses 7 games, note that the roster ABCD, ABEF, ACEG, ADFG, BCEG, BDFG, CDEF is an example of seven games satisfying the requirements. Hence G = 7, and moreover, P ≤ 7. It remains to verify that P ≥ 7, i.e., to verify there is no construction of seven games using fewer than 7 players. In order for seven games to take place, after the first game, the remaining (cid:0)4(cid:1) = 6 games must have exactly two players among {A, B, C, D} and two new players not among {A, B, C, D}. If there were only two new players (say, only E and F), then one cannot have them play in all six games. Hence a seventh player is necessary, so it follows that P ≥ 7. In summary, (G, P ) = (7, 7). ←→
summary: Tests construction and bounding of combinatorial designs with pairwise intersection constraints on 4-element subsets.

statement: Let △ABC be isosceles with AB = 1. Suppose that there is exactly one point C′ on BC, distinct from B and C, such that △ABC′ is also isosceles. Compute the sum of all distinct possible lengths AC.
solution: Let ℓ be the line BC for shorthand. Call a point on ℓ a potential location for C′ if two of AB, BC′, and C′A are equal. Call a potential location a valid location if, additionally, it is distinct from B and C. The problem statement requires exactly one valid location on ℓ. All possible potential locations are as follows: (cid:136) The potential locations with AB = AC′ are B and the reflection of B over the perpendicular from A to ℓ. Call this point W . (cid:136) The potential locations with BC′ = BA are the points on ℓ that are 1 unit away from B. Let X be the one on the same side of B as C, and Y be the one on the opposite side. (cid:136) The only potential location with C′A = C′B is the intersection of the perpendicular bisector of AB with ℓ, if it exists. Call this point Z. A Z Y W B C X Note that Y is always valid, and X is always distinct from B and Y . Thus the condition of a unique valid location is equivalent to W ∈ {B, C, Y }, X = C, and either Z does not exist or Z ∈ {B, C, Y }. First, observe that the condition that X = C is equivalent to BC = 1. The configuration is thus characterized by the angle θ between AB and BC. Next, analyze the condition on W . (cid:136) If W = B, then AB ⊥ ℓ. This gives θ = 90◦. (cid:136) If W = C, then △ABC is equilateral. This gives θ = 60◦. (cid:136) If W = Y , then θ is obtuse and BW = 1. So △ABW is equilateral, which gives θ = 120◦. Thus the condition on W is equivalent to θ ∈ {60◦, 90◦, 120◦}. Finally, analyze the condition on Z. (cid:136) If Z does not exist, then AB ⊥ ℓ. This gives θ = 90◦. (cid:136) Note that Z cannot equal B. (cid:136) If Z = C, then △ABC is equilateral. This gives θ = 60◦. (cid:136) If Z = Y , then θ is obtuse and BZ = 1. So △ABZ is equilateral, which gives θ = 120◦. Thus the condition on Z is equivalent to θ ∈ {60◦, 90◦, 120◦}. A A A Y B C = W = X = Z Y B = W C = X Y = W = Z B C = X Thus the condition of a unique valid location is equivalent to BC = 1 and m∠ABC ∈ {60◦, 90◦, 120◦}. This √ √ √ √ corresponds to AC being 1, 2, and 3, respectively. Thus the sum of all possible distances AC is 1+ 2+ 3.
summary: Find all side lengths AC of an isosceles triangle with exactly one valid isosceles sub-triangle point on BC.

statement: Silas lines up 12 fair coins and flips each of them. Marnie then removes any coin that shows the same face as one or both of its neighbors. Compute the probability that at most one coin remains.
solution: Assume without loss of generality that the first flip is heads. Then denote each subsequent flip by an S if it is the same as the previous flip and a D if it is different. Appending an S to each end of the resulting sequence, there will be at most one coin remaining if there is at most one instance of two consecutive Ds in the sequence. For a set of n coins, let a be the number of such sequences (of length n + 1) that contain exactly n one instance of two consecutive Ds, and b be the number of such sequences (of length n + 1) that contain no n two consecutive Ds. Then note that a = 0 and b = 1. For n > 2, it follows that 2 2 b = b + b + · · · + b + 1 n n−2 n−3 2 because a sequence of length n can be created from a sequence of length n − k (k ≥ 2), followed by k − 1 Ds and an S, or from the sequence SDD. . . DS. Similarly, a = b + a + a + · · · + a + 1, because a n n−1 n−2 n−3 2 sequence that has two consecutive Ds can be created in the same way as above, or by appending one more D to a sequence that does not have two consecutive Ds. Use this recursive formula to compute a and b for n = 3, 4, n n . . . , 12, ending with a = 240 and b = 89. The number of possible sequences is 211, so the probability is 329 . 12 12 2048 Alternate Solution: Each of the 212 sequences of H and T corresponds to a way to write 12 as a sum of the repeat-lengths of Hs or Ts. For example, HHHHTTTTHHTH becomes the ordered partition 12 = 4+4+2+1+1. Moreover, each ordered partition corresponds to two sequences of coins. Begin by computing the number of ordered partitions of n that have at most one 1. If there are no 1s, find the number of solutions to 12 = x + x + · · · + x , where x ≥ 2. For a fixed k (1 ≤ k ≤ 6), this is the number of nonnegative integer 1 2 k i solutions to 12 − 2k = y + y + · · · + y , which, by the sticks-and-stones method, equals (cid:0)11−k(cid:1) . Summing 1 2 k k−1 from k = 1, . . . , 6 gives 6 Ç å Ç å Ç å Ç å Ç å Ç å Ç å (cid:88) 11 − k 10 9 8 7 6 5 = + + + + + = 89. k − 1 0 1 2 3 4 5 k=1 (In fact, if 11 is replaced by the odd number m ≥ 1, this can be shown to equal the mth Fibonacci number, F . In this case, F = 89.) If there is one 1, find the number of solutions to 12 = x + x + · · · + x , where m 11 1 2 k some x = 1 and x ≥ 2 for i ̸= t. For fixed k, there are k ways of selecting t; then again, applying the t i sticks-and-stones method gives (cid:0)11−k(cid:1) solutions. Hence the total in this case is k−2 6 Ç å (cid:88) 11 − k k = 240. k − 2 k=2 In total, there are 89 + 240 = 329 ordered partitions in which at most a single element is 1. Thus the answer is 2·329 = 329 . 212 2048
summary: Tests counting sequences/ordered partitions of coin flips satisfying a neighbor-removal condition via recurrences and stars-and-bars.

statement: For each prime p, let U denote the set of ordered triples (a, b, c) ∈ {1, 2, . . . , p}3 for which p (cid:136) there exists an integer x for which p divides x2 − a, (cid:136) there exists an integer y for which p divides y3 − b, (cid:136) there exists an integer z for which p divides z5 − c, and (cid:136) p divides a + b + c. Compute the least prime p for which |U | > 2025. p
solution: Assume p > 5 is a prime, and work with a, b, c modulo p. The solution proceeds in three steps. Claim: |U | ≤ p (cid:0) p+1 (cid:1) . p 2 To prove the claim, note that a and b uniquely determine c. Further note that there are exactly p+1 choices of a, and there are at most p choices of b. This implies the result. Consequently |U | ≥ 2025 would imply p p(p + 1) > 2025 · 2, meaning p ≥ 67. Claim: If p ≡ 1 (mod h) for h ∈ {3, 5}, then |U | < (cid:0) p+1 (cid:1) · (cid:0) p−1 + 1 (cid:1) . p 2 h To prove the claim, consider that if h = 3, then there are at most p−1 + 1 cubes modulo p, thus at most p−1 + 1 3 3 values of b are allowed; then the value of a uniquely determines c. The argument is identical for h = 5, with the roles of b and c swapped. Thus the primes p = 67, p = 71, p = 73, and p = 79 can be ruled out because in these cases, p − 1 has factors of 3, 5, 3, and 3, respectively. On the other hand, note that U has in fact exactly 83 (cid:0) 83+1 (cid:1) = 3486 > 2025 83 2 triples because every residue modulo 83 is both a perfect cube and a perfect fifth power, so the answer is 83.
summary: Count ordered triples satisfying quadratic, cubic, and quintic residue conditions plus a sum divisibility condition modulo a prime.

statement: The trisectors of ∠A in △ABC intersect the circumcircle of the triangle at points D and E so that the points A, B, D, E, and C lie on the circumcircle in this order. Given that AB = 4, AD = 16, and AC = 14, compute BC.
solution: Let BC = a, AB = b, AC = c, AD = d, AE = e, BD = DE = EC = x, and BE = CD = y. A b = 4 c = 14 B C a e d = 16 y x x y D x E Using Ptolemy’s Theorem in quadrilaterals ABDE and ACED, it follows that (b + e)x = dy and (c + d)x = ey. Therefore y b + e c + d 4 + e 30 = = =⇒ = =⇒ e = 20. x d e 16 e Applying the Law of Cosines in triangles ABD and ADE yields Å ∠BAC ã b2 + d2 − x2 d2 + e2 − x2 cos = = . 3 2bd 2de Hence b(d2 + e2 − x2) = e(b2 + d2 − x2), so e(b2 + d2) − b(d2 + e2) 20(42 + 162) − 4(162 + 202) x2 = = = 176. e − b 20 − 4 √ √ Therefore x = 4 11, and because y = 3 , it follows that y = 6 11. Using Ptolemy’s Theorem in quadrilateral x 2 √ BDEC, it follows that ax + x2 = y2, so a = 5 11. Alternate Solution: Let ∠BAC = 3θ < 180◦. Because triangles ABC, ABD, and ACD have the same circumcircle (of radius 2R), by the Extended Law of Sines, it follows that BC = 2R sin 3θ, BD = 2R sin θ, and CD = 2R sin 2θ. Using Ptolemy’s Theorem in quadrilateral ABDC,, it follows that 4 · CD + 14 · BD = 16 · BC. Thus 8 sin 3θ = 2 sin 2θ + 7 sin θ. Using the triple and double angle identities for the sin function, it follows that 8(3 sin θ − 4 sin3 θ) = 2 sin θ cos θ + 7 sin θ ⇐⇒ 17 − 32 sin2 θ = 4 cos θ ⇐⇒ 17 − 32(1 − cos2 θ) = 4 cos θ ⇐⇒ 0 = 32 cos2 θ − 4 cos θ − 15 = (8 cos θ + 5)(4 cos θ − 3). Hence cos θ = 3 or cos θ = − 5 . However, because θ < 60◦, it follows that cos θ = 3 . Applying the Law of 4 8 4 Cosines in triangle BAC yields BC2 = 42 + 142 − 2 · 4 · 14 · cos 3θ Ç Å 3 ã3 3 å = 212 − 112 4 · − 3 · 4 4 = 275, √ so BC = 5 11.
summary: Tests Ptolemy's theorem and trig identities on a cyclic polygon formed by angle trisectors meeting the circumcircle.

statement: Compute the least integer N for which is an integer. (N + 3)(N + 4)(N + 5)
solution: Note that if any of N + 3, N + 4, and N + 5 is prime, then the given fraction will not be an integer because that prime will not divide N !. Therefore look for three consecutive composite numbers. The least three consecutive composite numbers are 8, 9, and 10, but if N + 5 = 10, then N = 5, and 5! = 120 < 720 = 8 · 9 · 10. The next least three consecutive composite numbers are 14, 15, 16, and because 11! = 11880 is an integer, 14·15·16 the answer is 11.
summary: Find the least integer N so that N! is divisible by the product of three consecutive integers (N+3)(N+4)(N+5).

statement: Compute the number of ways in which an 87 × 1 grid can be fully tiled with rectangles of size 8 × 1 and 7 × 1.
solution: The tiling must use at least (cid:6) 87 (cid:7) = 11 tiles, and at most (cid:4) 87 (cid:5) = 12 tiles. If there are 11 tiles, then 8 7 there must be ten 8 × 1 tiles and one 7 × 1 tile; there are (cid:0)11(cid:1) = 11 such tilings, because the order of the tiles matters. If there are 12 tiles, then there must be three 8 × 1 tiles and nine 7 × 1 tiles. In this case, there are (cid:0)12(cid:1) = 220 such tilings. Thus the total number of tilings is 11 + 220 = 231.
summary: Count tilings of an 87×1 grid with 8×1 and 7×1 tiles by casework on tile counts.

solution: Notice that 0.25 = 25 and 0.20 = 20 , so it follows that 1 > 5 → N < 99 . Thus N < 19.8, so the 99 99 N 99 5 answer is 19. (cid:112) √ (cid:112) √ √ 3 + 5 + 3 − 5 √

summary: Tests simplification of nested radicals by denesting and rationalizing the denominator.

statement: Similar right triangles DEF and XY Z have right angles at D and X, respectively. Let DF intersect XY at G, let DF intersect Y Z at H, and let DE intersect XZ at I. Segments EF and Y Z are parallel, and X lies on EF so that XG = ZI = DH. Given that Y G = F G = 8, compute the greater of [DEF ] and [XY Z]. E X I Z F G H D Y
solution: Let Y Z intersect DE at J, as shown below. E X I Z J F G H D Y Let XG = ZI = DH = x. By parallel lines, it follows that △XGF ∼ △Y GH ∼ △JDH ∼ △JIZ. Furthermore, because ZI = DH, it follows that △JDH ∼ = △JIZ. Thus JD = JI. Because XG = ID, it follows that JD = JI = x/2. Then XG/F G = JD/HD = 1/2, so x = 8 · 1 = 4. Now Y G/HG = 1/2, so HG = 16, and so DF = 28. Also XI = GD = 20, so XZ = 24. So the larger of △DEF and △XY Z is △DEF . To compute [DEF ], it suffices to find DE because DF is already known. Because EI/XI = 1/2, it follows that EI = 10, and so DE = 14. Thus [DEF ] = 1 · 28 · 14 = 196.
summary: Tests identifying similar and congruent triangles from parallel lines to find lengths and compute a triangle's area.

statement: Compute the least positive multiple of 7 (expressed as a base-ten number) that has exactly seven 1s in its binary representation.
solution: If a positive integer n has exactly seven 1s in its binary representation, it must be equal to 2a1 + 2a2 + · · · + 2a7 for distinct nonnegative integers a > a > · · · > a . The least possible value of a is 6. Check the possible values of a in increasing order. Suppose that a = 6. Then (a , a , . . . , a ) = (6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0). So n = 26 + 25 + · · · + 20 = 127. This is not a 1 1 2 7 multiple of 7. Suppose that a = 7. Then n = 27+26+· · ·+20−2b = 255−2b for some b ∈ {0, . . . , 6}. Then n ≡ 3−2b (mod 7). But 2b (mod 7) ∈ {1, 2, 4}, so n is not a multiple of 7. Suppose that a = 8. Then n = 28 + 27 + · · · + 20 − 2b − 2c = 511 − 2b − 2c for some distinct b, c ∈ {0, . . . , 7}. Then n ≡ −2b − 2c (mod 7). But 2b (mod 7) ∈ {1, 2, 4} and 2c (mod 7) ∈ {1, 2, 4}, so n is not a multiple of 7. Suppose that a = 9. Then n = 29 + 28 + · · · + 20 − 2b − 2c − 2d = 1023 − 2b − 2c − 2d for some distinct b, c, d ∈ {0, . . . , 8}. Then n ≡ 1 − 2b − 2c − 2d (mod 7). In order for n to be a multiple of 7, this requires 2b, 2c, and 2d (mod 7) to be equivalent to 2, 2, and 4 (mod 7) in some order. To find the least possible n, it suffices to make 2b + 2c + 2d as great as possible. The values of k ∈ {0, . . . , 8} such that 2k ≡ 2 (mod 7) are 1, 4, and 7. The values of k ∈ {0, . . . , 8} such that 2k ≡ 4 (mod 7) are 2, 5, and 8. Thus the greatest possible value of 2b + 2c + 2d under these conditions is 28 + 27 + 24. It follows that the least possible n is 1023 − 28 − 27 − 24 = 623.
summary: Find the least positive multiple of 7 whose binary representation contains exactly seven 1s.

statement: Let x satisfy log (log 2x) = 1 . Compute log x. 50(502) 2(5050) 100 50
solution: Let log x = y. Then x = 50y, so log 2x = x = 50y = 50y−50. Thus log (log 2x) = 50 2(5050) 5050 5050 50(502) 2(5050) log 50y−50 = y−50 . Setting this equal to 1 yields the equation y − 50 = 25, so y = 75. 50(502) 2500 100
summary: Tests manipulation of nested logarithms with different bases to solve for a logarithmic expression.

statement: The polynomial f has integer coefficients, and √ √ Ä ä f 20 + 2 + 5 = 2025. Compute the least possible positive value of f (1).
solution: Take the minimal polynomial1 of 20 + 2 + 5, namely g(X) = Ä (X − 5)2 − 22 ä2 − 160. √ √ (Note that the four roots of this polynomial are ± 20 ± 2 + 5.) It must divide f (X) − 2025, meaning that f (X) = 2025 + g(X) · q(X), for some polynomial q with integer coefficients. Thus f (1) = 2025 + g(1) · q(1). However, g(1) = Ä (1 − 5)2 − 22 ä2 − 160 = (16 − 22)2 − 160 = −124. Hence the necessary and sufficient condition for possible values of f (1) is f (1) ≡ 2025 ≡ 41 (mod 124). The answer is thus 41.
summary: Find the least positive f(1) for an integer-coefficient polynomial satisfying a nested radical evaluation, using the minimal polynomial and divisibility.

statement: Compute the number of permutations of the digits of 123456789 for which the odd digits are in increasing order.
solution: The positions of the odd digits determine their individual locations, and there are (cid:0)9(cid:1) = 9·8·7·6 = 126 5 4·3·2·1 ways to place them. The even digits can then be arranged in 4! = 24 ways for a total of 126 · 24 = 3024 permutations whose odd digits are in increasing order. 1The minimal polynomial of a complex number z is the least-degree monic polynomial with rational coefficients that has z as a root (if such a polynomial exists).
summary: Count permutations of 1–9 digits where the five odd digits appear in increasing order.

statement: Let △ABC be an acute triangle with incenter I. Suppose csc A, csc B, and csc C form an arithmetic progression with common difference 1 , and AI2, BI2, and CI2 form an arithmetic progression with common difference 25. Compute [ABC].
solution: Let a = BC, b = CA, c = AB, s = (a + b + c)/2, and let r be the inradius of △ABC. Note that AI2 − BI2 = (cid:0) (s − a)2 + r2(cid:1) − (cid:0) (s − b)2 + r2(cid:1) (cid:0) (cid:1) (cid:0) (cid:1) = (s − a) + (s − b) · (s − a) − (s − b) = c · (b − a) = cb − ca Å 1 1 ã = 2[ABC] · − sin A sin B = 2[ABC] · (csc A − csc B) . Hence AI2 − BI2 25 [ABC] = = = 250. 2(csc A − csc B) 2 · 1 Remark: This solution does not use the entire arithmetic progression. In fact, it shows that as long as csc A, csc B, csc C are an arithmetic progression, then the lengths AI2, BI2, CI2 are an arithmetic progression as well, with the common difference scaled by 2[ABC]. Consequently, even though the problem seems to specify four conditions, one of them is redundant. The unique choice of angles that yield the desired progression are ∠A ≈ 64.457◦, ∠B ≈ 59.691◦, ∠C ≈ 55.852◦.
summary: Tests the relationship between incenter distances, inradius, and the Law of Sines to connect two arithmetic progressions.

statement: Suppose m and n are integers that satisfy the system m + n2 + mn = 497 n + 2m2 = 546. Compute m + n. √
solution: The problem statement implies (n − m) + (2m2 − n2 − mn) = 546 − 497 = 49. Note that (m − n) can be factored from the left-hand side: (m − n)(2m + n − 1) = 49. So m − n and 2m + n − 1 must be (possibly negative) factors of 49 that multiply to 49. Furthermore, the sum of the two factors is 3m − 1 and thus must be equivalent to 2 mod 3. So (m − n, 2m + n − 1) is one of (1, 49), (7, 7), or (49, 1). In each case, (m, n) is determined by solving the systems of equations implied by the first two columns of the following table. m − n 2m + n − 1 m n 1 49 17 16 7 7 5 −2 49 1 17 −32 Now substitute back in to the original system. In the pair (m, n) = (5, −2), note that m and |n| are too small to satisfy the original system. If m = 17, then 2m2 = 578, so n must be −32 for the second of the given equations to hold. Then the first given equation also holds, so the answer is 17 + (−32) = −15. √
summary: Tests solving a nonlinear integer system by factoring a difference into divisors of 49 and using modular constraints.

statement: Compute the lattice point (a, b) closest to the origin for which (a − b) a + b = 999. min T
solution: First note that because a and b are integers, a + b must be a perfect square. Moreover, because the factor of (a − b) is multiplied by a positive integer, it follows that a − b is also a positive integer. Now consider the following table. a − b a + b 1 9992 3 3332 9 1112 27 372 37 272 111 92 333 32 999 12 The problem is to find the ordered pair (a, b) that minimizes a2 + b2. Now consider the pair of equations a − b = 333 and a + b = 9. This is satisfied by (a, b) = (171, −162). Note that unless a − b = 111 and a + b = 81, each of the other pairs of equations implied by the above table lead to a ≥ 171 and |b| ≥ 162. On the other hand, the pair of equations a − b = 111 and a + b = 81 is satisfied by (96, −15), which is the desired point because 962 + (−15)2 < 1712 + 1622. min T
summary: Find the lattice point closest to the origin satisfying a Diophantine equation involving a perfect-square factor, using divisor enumeration.

statement: Let S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}. Compute the number of nonempty subsets T of S for which ∈ S. |T |
solution: Suppose that |T | = 1. Then there is only one element in the subset T , and that element is the minimum of the subset. Thus there are 6 such subsets T . Suppose that |T | = 2. Then the minimum of T can be either 2 or 4. If min T = 2, then there are 4 other elements that could possibly be in T . If instead min T = 4, then there are 2 other elements that could possibly be in T . Thus there are 4 + 2 = 6 subsets T of this type. Suppose that |T | = 3. Then the minimum of T can be only 3. The other two possible elements of T can be chosen in (cid:0)3(cid:1) = 3 ways, so there are 3 subsets T of this type. If |T | > 3, then min T > 3, but then this implies that |T | ≤ 3, which contradicts the assumption that |T | > 3. Thus the final answer is 6 + 6 + 3 = 15.
summary: Count nonempty subsets of {1,...,6} where the minimum element equals the subset's size.

statement: Given that the product 20262025 × 20262023 is the fifteen-digit number 410549A165765B5, compute the ordered pair (A, B).
solution: Observing that 20262024 is a multiple of 3, express this product generally as (3n+1)(3n−1) = 9n2 −1 for some value of n. This means that 410549A165765B6 is a multiple of 9, and so the sum of its digits must be divisible by 9. Thus 4 + 1 + 5 + 4 + 9 + A + 1 + 6 + 5 + 7 + 6 + 5 + B + 6 + 1 = 59 + A + B must be a multiple of 9. Quick inspection of the product 25 · 23 = 575 implies that B must be equal to 7. Thus 66 + A must be 72, so A = 6, and the answer is (6, 7). Problem 2. Consider the following cross-number puzzle. No answer has a leftmost digit of 0. 1-Across: Sum of the cubes of the digits in 4-Across 4-Across: A perfect cube 5-Across: Prime with a digit sum of 16 1 A 2 B 3 C 4 D E F 1-Down: Its digits form an increasing arithmetic sequence 5 G H I 2-Down: A Fibonacci number 3-Down: Decimal representation of 1101000101 Compute A B C + D E F + G H I.
summary: Find missing digits in a product by using divisibility by 9 and last-digit inspection.

statement: Consider the following cross-number puzzle. No answer has a leftmost digit of 0. 1-Across: Sum of the cubes of the digits in 4-Across 4-Across: A perfect cube 5-Across: Prime with a digit sum of 16 1 A 2 B 3 C 4 D E F 1-Down: Its digits form an increasing arithmetic sequence 5 G H I 2-Down: A Fibonacci number 3-Down: Decimal representation of 1101000101 Compute A B C + D E F + G H I.
solution: Although one can convert the number in 3-Down (call it N ) to decimal, one could also estimate N > 29 + 28 > 700 , so as a decimal, the leftmost digit of N (which is C) must be at least 7. Because 2-Down and 4-Across share the same middle digit, it is useful to list three-digit Fibonacci numbers and perfect cubes. Note that 144 is a Fibonacci number and shares its middle digit with 343, a perfect cube. Note also that 610 is a Fibonacci number and shares its middle digit with the perfect cubes 216 and 512. However, the sums of the cubes of the digits of 216 and 512 (as required by 1-Across) are 225 and 134, respectively, and in both cases, the units digits are less than 7. Thus 2-Down is 144, 4-Across is 343, and 1-Across is 33 + 43 + 33 = 118. The clue for 1-Down implies that G = 5. Because 5-Across has a digit sum of 16, it follows that I = 16 − (5 + 4) = 7, and one can verify that 547 is indeed prime. The value of I can also be determined by adding up units digits of appropriate powers of 2. Thus A B C + D E F + G H I = 118 + 343 + 547 = 1008. statement: Compute the number of ways to tile the grid shown at right with 2 × 1 and 1 × 2 rectangles.
solution: It can be readily shown that the number of ways to tile a 2 × N rectangle with 2 × 1 rectangles is F , the (N + 1)st Fibonacci number (with F = 0 and F = 1). N+1 0 1 Accordingly, the problem can be broken down by how the four squares at the intersection of the horizontal and vertical 2 × 12 parts are tiled. If the four squares are tiled by two horizontal or vertical tiles, then there are (F )2 ways to tile the remaining 2 × 10 and 10 × 2 rectangles. If the squares are covered by one horizontal tile against the bottom boundary and two vertical tiles covering the other two squares (or similarly, one vertical tile against the rightmost boundary and two horizontal tiles covering the other two squares), then there are F ·F 11 10 ways to tile the remaining rectangles. Because F = 55 and F = 89, the answer is 2·892 +2·89·55 = 25632. 10 11 Problem 6. statement: Compute the least real number c such that the graphs of y = x2 + c and x = y2 + c intersect at an odd number of points.
solution: For most c ≥ 0, there are 2 intersection points for small c and none for large c. Because the two parabolas are reflections over the line y = x, the transition happens when the two parabolas intersect along the axis of reflection, so x = x2 + c has a unique solution, or x2 − x + c is a perfect square, which happens when c = 1/4. For c < 0, the number of intersections goes from 2 to 4, but without a transition of 3 intersection points. Instead there are two intersections when c ≥ −3/4 and four intersections when c < −3/4. One way to see this is that intersections happen when x = (x2 + c)2 + c, or 0 = x4 + 2cx2 − x + c2 + c = (x2 − x + c)(x2 + x + 1 + c). The first factor has a double root when c = 1/4, as already found, and the second has a double root when the discriminant 1 − 4 − 4c = 0, or c = −3/4. But when c = −3/4, the first factor x2 − x − 3/4 is 0 when x = −1/2 or 3/2, while the second factor x2 + x + 1/4 has its double root at x = −1/2, so the degree-four polynomial has a triple root at −1/2 and single root at 3/2 thus the parabolas intersect twice. Therefore an odd number of intersections only happens when c is 1 . Problem 7. An Alphabubu Blind Box is a box containing one tile, on which is one letter of the alphabet. When Glenn opens a box, the tile inside is equally likely to contain each of the 26 letters of the alphabet. Compute the expected number of boxes that Glenn would need to open in order to spell GLENN with his tiles.

statement: Let a and b be positive real numbers such that , ab, and are integers with sum 335. 2 1 + 1 a b Compute |a − b|.
solution: Note that AH = G2. Let n = gcd(A, H), so A and H are relatively prime positive integers whose n n product is a perfect square. Hence they must themselves be perfect squares; say p2 and q2. Henceforth, write A = np2, G = npq, H = nq2 for integers n and p ≥ q > 0. Thus n(p2 + pq + q2) = 5 · 67. Because p2 + pq + q2 = (p − q)2 + 3pq is 0 mod 3 or 1 mod 3, we must have p2 + pq + q2 = 67, and n = 5. The only possible (p, q) is (7, 2) so (A, G, H) = (245, 70, 20). Thus (a − b)2 = (a + b)2 − 4ab = (2A)2 − 4G2 = 4902 − 4 · 702 = 702(72 − 4) √ √ and so |a − b| = 70 5 · 9 = 210 5. Problem 10. Let △ABC be a nondegenerate acute triangle. statement: Let △ABC be a nondegenerate acute triangle. A student computes the six standard trigonometric functions (i.e., sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, and cot) of each of the three angles of △ABC, resulting in k distinct values. Compute the sum of all possible values of k.
solution: Before delving into casework, here are some observations that help in all cases. Define a new operator tot(t) = min{tan t, cot t}. Note that tot(t) < 1 for 0◦ < t < 90◦ and t ̸= 45◦. Next, let ϵ be the number of angles A, B, C which are equal to 45◦. Note that ϵ = 0 or ϵ = 1. Then k is ϵ less than twice the number of values in the following 3 × 3 matrix:   sin A cos A tot A M = sin B cos B tot B . sin C cos C tot C Note that within each column, two entries can only be equal only if the corresponding angles are equal. (For the tot column, this requires acuteness of the triangle.) Also, sin A = cos B and similar equations are impossible because ABC is an acute triangle; and sin A = cos A can only occur if A = 45◦. It will also be necessary to analyze the equations sin(x) = tot(x) and cos(x) = tot(x). For acute angles x, note that sin(x) = tot(x) occurs only if 45◦ < x < 90◦ and sin(x) = cot(x) = cos(x) ; this is equivalent to √ sin(x) cos2 x + cos x = 1, which occurs only when cos(x) = 1 (−1 + 5). In other words, sin(x) = tot(x) has a unique 2√ solution, henceforth denoted by ϕ = arccos (cid:0) 1 (−1 + 5) (cid:1) . (For concreteness, it turns out that ϕ ≈ 51.827◦.) By a symmetric analysis, cos(x) = tot(x) can only occur when x = 90◦ − ϕ. Proceed with case analysis as follows. Call a real number evil if it appears more than once in M in different columns, and does not occur because sin 45◦ = cos 45◦. Then the above properties imply that besides duplicate rows for an isosceles triangle, an evil number must appear exactly twice: once in the tot column and once in a sin or cos column. Let n be the number of evil numbers, and let r be the number of different rows (that is, r = 1, 2, 3 if ABC is equilateral, isosceles and non-equilateral, or scalene, respectively). Note that n ≤ r − ϵ because all evil numbers appear once in the last column and lie in the interval (0, 1). Then k = 2(3r − n − ϵ) − ϵ = 6r − 2n − 3ϵ. The table below shows the possible cases in consideration, together with a triangle achieving each possible configuration. In the cases where an angle is underspecified, the choice should be made “sufficiently generically”, so that no unexpected equalities arise. ϵ = 0 n = 0 n = 1 n = 2 n = 3 60◦ || || 60◦ 60◦ || Equilateral r = 1 k = 6 ϕ || || || || Isosceles Impossible r = 2 k = 12 k = 10 k = 8 ϕ ϕ Scalene ρ Impossible r = 3 k = 18 k = 16 k = 14 k = 12 ϵ = 1 n = 0 n = 1 n = 2 n = 3 45◦ || || Isosceles Impossible r = 2 k = 9 k = 7 ϕ Scalene 45◦ 45◦ Impossible r = 3 k = 15 k = 13 k = 11 In the exceptional case where (ϵ, n, r) = (0, 2, 3), the angle ϕ is defined so that 45◦ < ϕ < 90◦ and the equation sin (cid:0) 180◦ − (ϕ + ρ) (cid:1) = cot ρ holds. This is possible because if ρ were equal to 45◦, the right-hand side of the above equation would be greater while when ρ = 90◦, the left-hand side would be greater, so continuity guarantees the existence of one such ρ. (For concreteness, it turns out that ρ ≈ 45.217◦.) It remains to show that the cases marked “Impossible” indeed never occur. The basic heuristic argument is that in each of these cases, one could consider writing down a system of equations in terms of the angles. In all these cases, there is one more equation than variable, so the resulting equation should never have any solutions. Thus one can rule out these cases based solely on intuition; hence the sum of the possible values of k is 6 + 12 + 10 + 18 + 16 + 14 + 9 + 15 + 13 = 113. For completeness, a proof of the impossible cases is sketched below. In order to make the story more readable, machine-based numerical approximations are used to describe the solutions to one-variable equations, even though this does not constitute a rigorous proof. To write an airtight formal proof, the numerical approxi- mations should be replaced by working with the values of sin θ and cos θ directly, showing that the pairs of equations cannot hold simultaneously. The organizers of the competition stress that it is not expected that contestants would carry out these proofs. (cid:136) The case where ϵ = 1 and r = 2 is easy because any isosceles acute triangle with an angle equal to 45◦ must in fact have the other two angles equal to 67.5◦, i.e., there is only one triangle up to similarity, and it satisfies n = 0. (cid:136) Consider the case (ϵ, n, r) = (0, 2, 2). If any of the angles are equal to ϕ or 90◦ − ϕ, then n = 1. Otherwise, let A = 2θ, B = C = 90◦ − θ, where 0◦ < θ < 45◦. The angle θ would have to obey tot(90◦ − θ) ∈ {sin 2θ, cos 2θ} tot(2θ) ∈ {sin θ, cos θ} . However, the unique angle 0 < θ < 45◦ for which the first equation holds is θ ≈ 28.532◦ for which tan θ = cos(2θ); this does not satisfy the second equation. (cid:136) Consider the case (ϵ, n, r) = (1, 2, 3). If any of the angles are equal to ϕ or 90◦ − ϕ, then n = 1. Otherwise, let A = θ and B = 135◦ − θ, where 45◦ < θ < 90◦. In order for n ≥ 2 to occur, the angle θ would have to obey ß 1 ™ tot(θ) ∈ sin(135◦ − θ), cos(135◦ − θ), √ ß 1 ™ tot(135◦ − θ) ∈ sin θ, cos θ, √ . √ However, the only angles 45◦ < θ < 90◦ satisfying the first equation are θ = arctan( 2) ≈ 54.736◦ and θ ≈ 68.366◦, for which cot θ = cos(135◦ − θ). This does not satisfy the second equation. (cid:136) The case (ϵ, n, r) = (0, 3, 3) is the most difficult because the family of scalene triangles is parametrized by two variables. Therefore, even given numerical analysis, this case cannot be easily dispatched. However, handling this is also not necessary for the problem at hand! This case yields the value k = 12, which was already covered by (ϵ, n, r) = (0, 0, 2). Therefore, while this case does turn out to be impossible, proving the impossibility is irrelevant to the final answer.
summary: Count distinct values among six trig functions of each angle in an acute triangle, summing all possible totals.

statement: Given that x and y are real numbers such that x2 + y2 = 202 and x − y = 6, compute |x + y|.
solution: Note that (x − y)2 = x2 − 2xy + y2 = 36. Because x2 + y2 = 202, it follows that 2xy = 166. Then √ √ note that (x + y)2 = x2 + 2xy + y2 = 202 + 166 = 368, hence |x + y| = 368 = 4 23. Problem 2. Let P be a polynomial each of whose coefficients is either 0, 2, or 6. Given that P (4) = 2026, compute P (10).

statement: Let P be a polynomial each of whose coefficients is either 0, 2, or 6. Given that P (4) = 2026, compute P (10).
solution: Let Q(x) = 1 P (x), so Q(x) = a xn + a xn−1 + · · · + a , where a ∈ {0, 1, 3} for all 0 ≤ i ≤ n. 2 n n−1 0 i Then Q(4) = a 4n + a 4n−1 + · · · + a = 1013. Note that a ≡ 1013 mod 4, and hence a = 1. Similarly, n n−1 0 0 0 4a + a and 16a + 4a + a are congruent to 1013 modulo 16 and 64, respectively, which yields a = 1 and 1 0 2 1 0 1 a = 3. Continuing in this fashion, note that this process is precisely computing the base-4 representation of P (4); that is, the coefficients of Q will be the digits of 1013 when written in base 4. Because 1013 = 33311 , it follows that Q(x) = 3x4 + 3x3 + 3x2 + x + 1, hence P (10) = 2 · Q(10) = 66622. Problem 3. statement: Jannik rolls two standard fair six-sided dice and tells Carlos the product of the two numbers rolled. Carlos guesses the sum of the two numbers rolled, by choosing the most likely sum based on the product Jannik told him. Compute the probability that Carlos guesses correctly.
solution: If the product is a number that can be expressed in only one way as the product of two factors no greater than 6, then Carlos can determine what the two die numbers are and will be able to guess the sum precisely. If the product is 4 (2 · 2 or 1 · 4) or 6 (2 · 3 or 1 · 6) or 12 (2 · 6 or 3 · 4), then Carlos should guess one of the possible sums. Either sum is equally likely, except in the case where the product is 4. Then Carlos should guess that the sum is 5, not 4, because it is twice as likely that the rolls are 1 and 4, rather than 2 and 2. Those three cases occur with probabilities 3 , 4 , and 4 , respectively, with Carlos guessing correctly 2 , 36 36 36 3 1 , and 1 of the time. The remaining 25 of the time, Carlos always guesses correctly. Thus Carlos can guess 2 2 36 correctly with probability 31 . Problem 4. Compute sin2 42◦ + sin2 78◦ − sin 42◦ sin 78◦.
summary: Tests enumeration of dice products and conditional probability to find Carlos's correct-guess probability.

statement: Compute sin2 42◦ + sin2 78◦ − sin 42◦ sin 78◦.
solution: Note that the given expression equals sin2(60◦ − A) + sin2(60◦ + A) − sin(60◦ − A) sin(60◦ + A), where A = 18◦. By a double-angle identity and then addition/subtraction identities, sin2(60◦ − A) + sin2(60◦ + A) = 1 (cid:0) (1 − cos(120◦ − 2A)) + (1 − cos(120◦ + 2A)) (cid:1) cos(120◦ − 2A) + cos(120◦ + 2A) = 1 − (cos 120◦ cos 2A + sin 120◦ sin 2A) + (cos 120◦ cos 2A − sin 120◦ sin 2A) = 1 − cos 2A = 1 + . By a product-to-sum identity, cos(−2A) − cos 120◦ sin(60◦ − A) sin(60◦ + A) = cos 2A 1 = + . 2 4 Hence the required expression is 1 + cos 2A − ( cos 2A + 1 ) = 3 (independent of A). 2 2 4 4 Alternate Solution: Let x = sin 42◦, y = sin 78◦, a = cos 42◦, and b = cos 78◦. Then note that sin 120◦ = √ sin(42◦ + 78◦) = bx + ay = 3 , and cos 120◦ = ab − xy = − 1 . Square both equations to obtain 2 2 b2x2 + 2abxy + a2y2 = a2b2 − 2abxy + x2y2 = . Subtract these two equations and factor to obtain (x2 − a2)(b2 − y2) + 4abxy = . Because a2 + x2 = b2 + y2 = 1, it follows that Å 1 ã 1 (2x2 − 1)(1 − 2y2) + 4xy xy − = , 2 2 or equivalently, 2x2 + 2y2 − 1 − 2xy = 1 , from which x2 + y2 − xy = 3 . Note that this would still hold if 42◦ 2 4 and 78◦ were replaced by any two angles summing to 120◦. Alternate Solution: Let x and y be the values of the angles 42◦ and 78◦ in radians, respectively. The sin3 x + sin3 y desired quantity is . Let α = eix and β = eiy. Then sin x + sin y 1 1 Å 1 ã 1 Å 1 ã sin x = (eix − e−ix) = α − and sin y = β − . 2i 2i α 2i β Then note that x + y = 2 3 π , so αβ = ei· 2 3 π , which is a cube root of unity, thus (αβ)3 = 1 and α3 = β 1 3 . This implies 1 ñÅ 1 ã3 Å 1 ã3ô sin3 x + sin3 y = − α − + β − 8i α β 1 Å 3 1 3 1 ã = − α3 − 3α + − + β3 − 3β + − 8i α α3 β β3 1 Å 3 3 ã = − −3α + − 3β + 8i α β 3 ÅÅ 1 ã Å 1 ãã = α − + β + 8i α β = (sin x + sin y). Thus the desired quantity is 3 , and in fact is independent of the actual angles, provided that their sum is 120◦. Problem 5. Compute the greatest possible number of sides of a convex polygon whose angle measures (in degrees) in some order are distinct consecutive integers.
summary: Tests simplification of a trigonometric expression using double-angle and product-to-sum identities.

statement: Compute the greatest possible number of sides of a convex polygon whose angle measures (in degrees) in some order are distinct consecutive integers.
solution: Let n be the number of sides of the polygon. In degrees, the average angle will be a = 180 − 360 , n so that will be the median of the list of consecutive integer measures. If a is an integer, then n must be odd, and the angles will be a, a ± 1, a ± 2, . . . , a ± n−1 . For convexity, 180 > a + n−1 = 180 − 360 + n−1 , which 2 2 n 2 simplifies to n(n − 1) < 720, so n ≤ 27 is a divisor of 360. The odd divisors of 360 are divisors of 45, and the greatest of these less than 27 is 15. However, there is also the possibility that n is even and a is a half-integer, in which case the angles will be a ± 1 , a ± 3 , . . . , a ± n−1 . For convexity, 180 > 180 − 360 + n−1 , which again 2 2 2 n 2 simplifies to n(n − 1) < 720, so n ≤ 27 and 360 is a half-integer, thus n is 16 times an odd divisor of 45. The n only such number is 16, which is greater than 15 and is thus the greatest possible number of sides. Problem 6. Suppose that △P QR has P Q = QR = 2026 and P R = 4051. Let △ABC have integer side lengths and m∠C ≤ m∠R. Compute the minimum possible perimeter of △ABC.
summary: Find the greatest number of sides of a convex polygon with distinct consecutive integer angle measures.

statement: Suppose that △P QR has P Q = QR = 2026 and P R = 4051. Let △ABC have integer side lengths and m∠C ≤ m∠R. Compute the minimum possible perimeter of △ABC.
solution: Let a = BC, b = AC, and c = AB. Assume, without loss of generality, that ∠C is the smallest angle in △ABC, and then that ∠A is the largest, so that c ≤ b ≤ a. By the Law of Cosines, cos C = a2+b2−c2 , and 2ab reducing c while holding a and b fixed will always (provided this does not violate the Triangle Inequality) result in a triangle with smaller perimeter and a smaller ∠C, so assume that b + c = a + 1. The perimeter of ABC is a + b + c = 2a + 1, so it suffices to minimize a. Substitute for c in the Law of Cosines, obtaining cos C = a2+b2−(a−b+1)2 , which simplifies to 1 + 1 − 1 − 1 . It follows that cos C ≤ 1 − 1 because b ≤ a. Applying 2ab a b 2ab 2a2 the Law of Cosines to △P QR gives cos R = 20262+40512−20262 = 4051 . If ∠C ≤ ∠R, then cos C ≥ cos R, so 2·2026·4051 4052 1 ≤ 1 . This gives a2 ≥ 2026, and because a is an integer, a ≥ 46. The minimum possible perimeter of 2a2 4052 △ABC is then 2 · 46 + 1 = 93. Problem 7. In triangle ABC, a circle that is tangent to side BC intersects side AB at points P and Q, where P is the point closer to A, and intersects side AC at points R and S, where R is the point closer to A, as shown. Given that AB = 13, AC = 15, AP = 4, AQ = 9, and AR = 3, compute BC. A P R Q S B C
summary: Find the minimum integer-sided triangle perimeter subject to an angle constraint using Law of Cosines.

statement: In triangle ABC, a circle that is tangent to side BC intersects side AB at points P and Q, where P is the point closer to A, and intersects side AC at points R and S, where R is the point closer to A, as shown. Given that AB = 13, AC = 15, AP = 4, AQ = 9, and AR = 3, compute BC. A P R Q S B C
solution: By the Power of a Point Theorem, AR · AS = AP · AQ, so 3AS = 4 · 9 and AS = 12. Hence BQ = 13 − 9 = 4 and CS = 15 − 12 = 3. Let D be the point where the circle intersects BC. Applying √ Power of a Point from B and C, respectively, BD2 = BQ · BP , so BD = 4 · 9 = 6 and CD2 = CS · CR, so √ CD = 3 · 12 = 6. Thus BC = BD + CD = 6 + 6 = 12. Problem 8. The 60 students of the West Coast ARML team and the 120 students of the East Coast ARML team are at a party. A total of 1800 handshakes occur between a West Coast student and an East Coast student, with no pair of people shaking hands more than once and no other handshakes occurring. For each pair of students at the party, the coaches record how many students shook hands with both. Compute the minimum possible average of the (cid:0)180(cid:1) numbers recorded by the coaches.
summary: Uses Power of a Point from each vertex to find segment lengths and compute BC.

statement: The 60 students of the West Coast ARML team and the 120 students of the East Coast ARML team are at a party. A total of 1800 handshakes occur between a West Coast student and an East Coast student, with no pair of people shaking hands more than once and no other handshakes occurring. For each pair of students at the party, the coaches record how many students shook hands with both. Compute the minimum possible average of the (cid:0)180(cid:1) numbers recorded by the coaches.
solution: It suffices to compute the sum of the numbers recorded; that is, the sum over all pairs of students of the number of students that shook hands with both. Consider the number of times each student is counted in this sum. If student s shook hands with d people, then s is counted once in (cid:0)ds (cid:1) different terms of the sum. s 2 Thus the sum is equal to Ç å (cid:88) d s , s where s ranges across all students at the party. Break the sum up into two parts, one sum over the West Coast (WC) students and one sum over the East Coast (EC) students. The sum over the West Coast is Ç å (cid:88) d s = 1 (cid:88) d2 − 1 (cid:88) d . 2 2 s 2 s s∈WC s∈WC s∈WC By the Cauchy–Schwarz Inequality, it follows that (cid:32) (cid:33) (cid:32) (cid:33) (cid:32) (cid:33)2 (cid:88) (cid:88) (cid:88) 1 d2 ≥ d , s s s∈WC s∈WC s∈WC so Ç å (cid:32) (cid:33)2 (cid:88) d s ≥ 1 (cid:88) d − 1 (cid:88) d . 2 2 · 60 s 2 s s∈WC s∈WC s∈WC By grouping the handshakes by which West Coast student is involved, observe that (cid:88) d s s∈WC is precisely the total number of handshakes, which is 1800. It follows that Ç å (cid:88) d s ≥ 18002 − 1800 = 900 · 29. 2 2 · 60 2 s∈WC Similarly, it follows that Ç å (cid:88) d s ≥ 18002 − 1800 = 900 · 14. 2 2 · 120 2 s∈EC Thus the average number recorded is Ç å 1 (cid:88) d s ≥ 1 (900 · 29 + 900 · 14) = 430 . (cid:0)180(cid:1) 2 90 · 179 179 2 s It remains to check that this is attainable. Equality in the Cauchy–Schwarz applications requires d to be s constant across all West Coast students and constant across all East Coast students. Divide the party into 4 groups, each with 15 West Coast students and 30 East Coast students. Within each group, suppose each of the 15 · 30 possible handshakes occur. Then 1800 total handshakes occur. Then d is 30 for each of the West s Coast students and 15 for each of the East Coast students, which gives an equality case. Thus the minimum possible average is 430 . Problem 9. Compute the second least positive integer n such that n + 1 and 5n + 1 are perfect squares.
summary: Minimize the average number of mutual handshake partners over all pairs, using double counting and Cauchy-Schwarz.

statement: Compute the second least positive integer n such that n + 1 and 5n + 1 are perfect squares.
solution: Do casework on the value of n + 1. The least positive integer n such that n + 1 is a perfect square is 3. For this value, 5·3+1 = 42. The number n+1 is also a perfect square for n = 8, 15, 24, 35, . . . , and the respective values of 5n + 1 are 41, 76, 121, 176, . . . . Noting that 121 = 112, it follows that n = 24 is the desired value of n. Alternate Solution: Let n+1 = x2 and 5n+1 = y2 for positive integers x and y. Noting that n = x2 −1 from the first equation, this can be substituted into the second equation to get 5(x2 − 1) + 1 = y2 or y2 − 5x2 = −4. The Lucas numbers L , defined by L = 2, L = 1, and L = L + L for n ≥ 2 and the Fibonacci n 0 1 n n−1 n−2 numbers F , defined by F = 1, F = 1, F = F + F for n ≥ 2 have the relationship L2 − 5F 2 = 4(−1)n, n 0 1 n n−1 n−2 n n meaning that the solutions to this problem correspond to the odd-indexed values of these sequences. The first pairs are (1, 1), (2, 4), and (5, 11), and the corresponding values of n are 0, 3, and 24. Problem 10. Let △ABC be a triangle with incenter I and incircle ω which is tangent to sides BC, CA, and AB at D, E, and F , respectively. Let T D be a diameter of ω. Suppose that the angle bisector of ∠BAC splits △ABC into two triangles, one of which is isosceles with vertex angle at A. Given that T D = 4 and [F IT ] − [T IE] = 35 , compute AI · [F IE].
summary: Find the second least positive integer n where both n+1 and 5n+1 are perfect squares, using a Pell-like equation and Lucas/Fibonacci identities.

Suppose that the angle bisector of ∠BAC splits △ABC into two triangles, one of which is isosceles with vertex angle at A. Given that T D = 4 and [F IT ] − [T IE] = 35 , compute AI · [F IE].
